planting.note: Plant bulbs 3-4 times their diameter deep in very well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, neutral to slightly alkaline soil in fall for spring flowering; Stars of Persia requires excellent drainage and dislikes wet soils, especially in summer when the bulbs are dormant. The enormous dried seed heads persist ornamentally for months and are excellent for drying. Allow the foliage to die back naturally after flowering.

directions: Plant bulbs 4-6 inches deep in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile soil in full sun; Stars of Persia produces some of the most spectacular flower heads of any ornamental allium — enormous, perfectly spherical umbels reaching 8-12 inches across that are composed of scores of individual starry, metallic silvery-purple to amethyst-pink florets, each on its own slender stem radiating outward from the center like a perfect starburst or fireworks explosion. The overall effect of a fully open flower head is unlike anything else in the spring garden — a huge, airy, three-dimensional sphere of metallic purple stars that seems to float above the ground. After the flowers fade, the seed heads dry in place and remain ornamental for months — often through the entire summer and into fall — their silver-gray skeletal structure providing exceptional structural interest. The florets of the dried head are used in dried flower arrangements. Plant in drifts for maximum impact; best combined with late-season perennials that will cover the fading foliage.

planting.note: Plant bulbs 3-4 inches deep in moist, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to part shade in fall for spring flowering; camas naturally grows in wet meadows and is one of the few spring bulbs that tolerates and even prefers consistently moist to wet soils. It naturalizes freely in moist conditions. The blue-violet flowers differ from the creamy-white to pale-blue C. leichtlinii, appearing more intensely colored and on shorter, more open spikes.

directions: Plant bulbs 3-4 inches deep in moist, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to part shade; camas is one of the most historically significant North American native bulbs — the protein-rich, starchy bulbs were a primary food staple for many Pacific Northwest indigenous peoples, who harvested them from the vast wet meadows and camas prairies that once covered parts of Oregon, Idaho, and surrounding regions, roasting or boiling them, or pit-cooking them for extended periods to convert the inulin starch to sweeter fructose. Camas prairies were carefully managed and harvested sustainably for generations. Tragically, the introduction of European livestock grazing destroyed most historic camas meadows. The flowers are beautiful — brilliant, electric, vivid blue-violet to deep violet-blue in elongated, loose spikes that naturalizes into drifts in moist meadow conditions. Distinct from the taller, paler C. leichtlinii by its more intensely blue-violet flowers and more open spike arrangement. One of the finest spring bulbs for naturalizing in moist, partly shaded meadow and garden settings.

planting.note: Sow seeds in lean, very well-drained, neutral to alkaline, gravelly or sandy soil in full sun; Hartweg's sundrops is closely related to evening primroses but classified separately. It thrives in the rocky limestone soils of central Texas and is extremely drought- and heat-tolerant once established. In zones 8-9 it grows as a subshrubby perennial; in zone 5-7 it is fully herbaceous and more compact.

directions: Sow seeds or plant small container plants in lean, very well-drained, neutral to alkaline, rocky or sandy soil in full sun; Hartweg's sundrops is a Texas Hill Country native producing a brilliant, long-lasting display of large, four-petaled, clear lemon-yellow flowers that open each evening and typically remain open through the following morning — giving much better garden visibility than pure evening primroses that close by midday. The flowers are 1-2 inches across, bright as sundrops, and produced nearly continuously from April through October in favorable conditions — an extraordinarily long bloom season. The plant forms a low, spreading, somewhat woody-based perennial mound of narrow, willow-like leaves. Native to the rocky, limestone soils of Texas, New Mexico, and northern Mexico, it is superbly adapted to the dry, alkaline, well-drained soils of the Texas Hill Country and adapts well to similar conditions elsewhere. One of the best-kept secrets of drought-tolerant native horticulture — remarkable persistence and bloom for a compact, low-maintenance native perennial.

planting.note: Plant bulbs 3 inches deep in very well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, neutral to slightly alkaline soil in fall; Ocean Grape Hyacinth is distinguished from common grape hyacinth (Muscari armeniacum) by its distinctive bicolor flowers — the lower florets are bright, cobalt blue while the upper florets are white or pale blue, creating a sharp two-toned color division. The cultivar Ocean Magic is the most widely available bicolor selection.

directions: Plant bulbs 3 inches deep in very well-drained, lean to moderately fertile soil in full sun; ocean grape hyacinth is the most visually distinctive grape hyacinth, producing compact spikes with a sharply defined two-tone appearance quite unlike the uniform blue of common grape hyacinth — the lower portion of each spike is composed of the typical bright cobalt-blue, closed urn-shaped florets of a grape hyacinth, while the upper quarter to third of the spike bears small, white to pale-blue, open florets that provide an abrupt, clean color transition. The overall effect of a clump in full bloom — dozens of blue-and-white bicolored spikes packed together — is very eye-catching and distinctive. The cultivar Ocean Magic is the standard commercial form of this effect and is widely available as named bulbs. Like all grape hyacinths, it naturalizes freely over time, multiplying by offsets to create expanding drifts. Outstanding when massed under early spring bulbs or planted in drifts with white tulips or yellow narcissus for a spring color combination.

planting.note: Plant bulbs 2-3 inches deep in any well-drained to moderately moist, neutral to slightly acidic soil in part shade to full sun; golden garlic is unusually shade-tolerant for an ornamental allium and will naturalize under deciduous trees where it receives good spring light before the canopy fills in. It multiplies freely to form colonies and the bulbs can be divided and replanted after the foliage dies back in summer.

directions: Plant bulbs 2-3 inches deep in any well-drained to moderately moist soil in part shade to full sun; golden garlic is the most shade-tolerant of the ornamental alliums — most of which require full sun — naturalizing successfully under deciduous trees where it receives bright spring light before the tree canopy leafs out, then rests dormant through the summer shade. The flowers are bright, clear, golden-yellow — very unusual in the allium genus where most ornamental species are purple, pink, or white — carried in loose, flat-topped umbels in May and June above two broad, gray-green, strap-like leaves with a light garlic scent when bruised. The plant naturalizes freely by offsets, expanding over time into attractive drifts that provide cheerful yellow spring color in the partly shaded garden. Native to rocky slopes and woodland margins of Spain, southern France, and North Africa, it has been cultivated in European gardens since at least the 16th century as 'lily leek.' Excellent for naturalizing at the woodland edge, in drifts under deciduous shrubs, or in massed plantings among hostas and other shade perennials.
